city yeah and
uh the person chasing him the person organizing a whole army to kill him yeah it is his own son
it is a total tragedy it is like a play by shakespeare but you know it was written thousands
of years before shakespeare was even born yeah it is probably one of the saddest pictures in
the entire source text it really is so welcome back to the deep dive we are so glad you are

story
of betrayal of deep sadness but also of just incredible loyalty we are doing a deep dive into

we are going to use a special tool to understand it better we are looking at the source material
from the scholar robert alter he wrote a very famous book called the art of biblical narrative
that is right and robert alter um absalom king david's son chapter 15 starts with absalom
making a very big very public move he really does and it is a very visual move the text tells us
absalom got for himself a carriage and horses and 50 runners to go before him
50 runners yeah 50 men running ahead of his carriage i mean that seems incredibly excessive
he is just a prince right why on earth does he need 50 men running in front of him well you have
to think about the visual image here in ancient times especially in the rocky hills where jerusalem
is located people usually rode mules or donkeys right very simple animals exactly even the king
rode a mule it was considered a humble steady animal but a chariot a big carriage pulled by
horses that was a wagon that was a wagon that was a wagon that was a wagon that was a wagon that was a
war machine like a piece of military equipment yes it was basically the ancient version of a tank
oh wow so if you see someone driving a tank down the main street and they have 50 security guards
in matching uniforms running in front of them you pay attention you definitely pay attention oh
absalom is putting on a massive show he is acting exactly like a king before he is actually the king
he is loudly telling the people look at me i am powerful i am strong i am the future of this
country he is basically building a war machine and he is actually building a war machine and he is
actually building a war machine and he is actually building a war machine and he is actually building
his brand he is making himself look incredibly important to the average person on the street
that is a great way to put it he is building a brand based on power and modern strength
but the thing is he does not just rely on the cool chariot yeah he also uses a very specific
very clever psychological technique to get the regular people to like him right the text
describes what we can call the man at the gate technique can you tell us about how that works
sure so in ancient walled cities the city gate was not just a heavy door to keep
enemies out it was the center of public life like a town square exactly it was like the courthouse
and the city hall combined if you had a legal problem maybe your neighbor stole your sheep
or you had a big disagreement about who owned a certain field you went to the city gate to find
a judge to decide who was right and the story says absalom goes there very early in the morning he
just stands right by the road leading into the gate yes and this is exactly where robert alters
idea about the importance of dialogue between the city gate and the city hall and the city hall is
becomes so clear we learn about absalom's true character not just by what the narrator tells us
about him but by what absalom actually says out loud to the people we have to pay attention to
his exact words we really do i have the text right here so when someone comes up the road with a
legal problem absalom stops them and asks where they're some he acts super friendly and then he
says this line he says see your cause is good and right but there is no one from the king to hear you
let us just pause right there that is
such a brilliant and totally dangerous sentence why is it dangerous i mean on the surface it
sounds really nice he is validating them look closely at the two separate things he does in that
one sentence first he flatters the person he says your cause is good but he does not even hear the
other side of the story he does not look at any evidence or call any witnesses he just immediately
tells the person you are right you win and as we know everyone loves to hear that they are right
that is very true everyone loves to hear that they are right that is very true everyone loves to hear
that they are right that is very true everyone loves to hear that they are right that is very true
if i go to a judge with the problem and the judge tells me i am right before the trial even starts
i am gonna really like that judge exactly it creates this instant powerful connection but
then look at the second half of his sentence he says but there is no one from the king to
hear you oh wow he is planting a seed of doubt yes he is essentially saying
the system is broken my father king david does not care about your problems
he is too busy he is getting too old he has not set up a proper system to actually help you get
justice
so he is completely blaming the current administration he is telling the regular
people that the government is failing them yes and then naturally once he has created
this feeling of anger and frustration he offers himself as the only solution he says oh that i
were made judge in the land then every man who has any suit or cause might come to me
and i would do him justice if i were in charge i would fix it all exactly that is just a classic
politician's speech it is timeless we see this exact same strategy in politics today and we are
reading about it happening three thousand years ago he creates the problem by saying the king is
too busy and then he presents himself as the wonderful savior who will listen and he goes out
of his way to not act like a distant snobby royal does he not at all the text says when people try
to bow down to him to show respect he would stop them he would reach out grab their hand and
actually kiss them this is the common man touch in those days you were absolutely supposed to bow to
a prince it was the law but absalom says no no do not bow to me we are equals i am your friend he
is breaking down that big barrier between the royal family and the normal citizens he is making
every single person he meets feel incredibly special yes and the text uses a very famous
very specific phrase here to describe the final result of all this hard work at the gate i know
the one you mean it says so absalom stole the hearts of the men of israel now that phrase stole
the hearts of the men of israel in english that sounds very romantic and poetic like a love song
but what does it actually mean in the original hebrew language that is a really great question
because it is not about romance at all in ancient hebrew thinking the heart the word is lev
is not just about your feelings or your emotions the heart is the center of your will it is the
center of your mind your logic and your decision making it is where you make your choices oh i see
so he did not just make the people have a crush on him he literally
stole their loyalty he stole their vote precisely to steal a heart means he took something that
rightfully did not belong to him their loyalty legally and morally belonged to the king to david
but absalom tricked them into giving it to him instead so it is theft yes he did not earn their
loyalty through years of hard work or brave service to the country he just took it through
charm cheap tricks and really smart manipulation okay so this slow manipulation goes on for a long
time the text says 40 years though some historical manuscripts say four years but
it is a long period of waiting it is a long game right absalom is standing at the gate
stealing hearts slowly building his secret army of supporters and then everything shifts which
brings us to part two of our story the conspiracy yes the plot moves out into the open and this
takes place at a city called hebron now geography is super important here we really need to
understand the map to get why this matters okay lay it out for us what is hebron hebron is the old
city it is where david first became king many years ago before he ever conquered jerusalem
so it is a very historic very holy place for the people that makes sense and absalom goes to his
father king david and asks for official permission to travel there but he obviously does not say hey
dad i am going to hebron to start a massive rebellion and take your throne no he tells a lie
and it is a very specific religious lie he says please let me go and pay my vow
which i have vowed to the lord a vow is like a very serious promise to god or a
exactly he tells his father that he made a sacred promise to god while he was living far away from
home in a place called gishur and now he needs to go to the holy city of hebron to worship and
fulfill that promise that is pretty twisted honestly he is using religious worship as a total
cover-up for high treason it really shows us the dark character of absalom he is perfectly willing
to use god's name and his father's respect for god to hide his plot to kill his own father it is
incredibly cynical now here is where i want to use that tool from robert alter that we talked about
earlier the tool of silence yes this is a perfect place for it because i want to look at david's
response to this lie absalom asks to go and david just says two words he says go in peace and that is
it this is a textbook example of narrative reticence reticence right that is just a fancy
academic word meaning the writer is intentionally holding back information from the reader
the text absolutely does not tell us what to do
it is just blank right is david suspicious does he notice the 50 runners and the chariot
does he know absalom is lying to his face it honestly feels like he does not know anything
that is the effect of the silence it suggests that david is completely in the dark or perhaps
he is just in deep denial you have to remember absalom is the son he deeply loves right a
father's blind spot exactly the writer leaves it blank so he can really feel the tragedy of david's
ignorance
here he actually gives a fatherly blessing to the son who is actively going off to try and kill him
he says go in peace while absalom is literally preparing for war
that is just heartbreaking when you think about it so assalon goes to hebron
and as soon as he gets there he sends secret spies out to all the different tribes of israel
with a special code signal yes the trap is springing the code is as soon as you hear the
sound of the trumpet then say absalom is king in hebron it is a highly coordinated coup it is a
rapid military takeover and you have to notice who he brings with him to hebron the text says he
took 200 men from jerusalem but the text adds a very specific detail about these 200 guys
it says they went in their simplicity they knew absolutely nothing about the rebellion this is
another absolute master stroke of manipulation by absalom he invites 200 very important influential
people to a sacrificial feast so they just think they were going to a big religious barbecue exactly
they think it is just a party they have no idea they are accidentally joining a violent revolution
why would he do that why bring innocent people into a war zone think about the optics think
about how it looks to everyone else watching if you are just a regular person living in hebron
and you see absalom arrive in town with 200 of the most important government
leaders from the capital city what are you gonna think oh i see you're gonna think wow
everyone supports him the whole government is with him this is official exactly
he uses their good reputation to make his illegal rebellion look completely legitimate it gives him
instant credibility with the local people but there is one person who was not innocent in all
of this there is one guy who joined the rebellion completely on purpose a man named ahithophel this
is the really heavy blow for david ahithophel was david's personal counselor his top advisor later
in the text it actually says that asking ahithophel for advice was like asking the word of god
he was a genius and he just switched his sides he betrays david and joins absalom and this changes
everything this makes the conspiracy truly strong before this absalom just had the charm the fancy
chariot and the angry people but ahithophel has the brains strategy right with ahithophel involved
this is no longer just a disorganized riot it is a serious deadly threat to the throne
ahithophel knows all of david's military secrets he knows david's weaknesses and that terrible reality
leads us right into part three the news finally breaks a messenger comes running to david in
jerusalem and the message is simple and totally terrifying the messenger says the hearts of the
men of israel have gone after absalom there is that phrase again the hearts are gone
and david's reaction to this news is immediate very fast he does not say okay let us get the
army ready to fight he does not say lock the huge city gates and prepare for a siege
he just looks around and says arise
and let us flee he decides to run away immediately why well it is a very controversial decision some
people reading this might just call it pure cowardice why does the great warrior king the
man who killed goliath just run away without a fight but you have to look at his actual reasoning
in the text what does he say he says to his servants let us flee lest he overtake us quickly
and bring down evil upon us and smite the city with the edge of the sword he wants to save the
city yes if david stays in jerusalem and tries to fight the city he will not be able to save the city
fight his own capital city becomes a bloody battlefield innocent families will die homes
and buildings will burn to the ground a total civil war right in the streets exactly so david
consciously chooses to become a homeless refugee he chooses to lose his nice house his comfort and
his dignity all just to save his capital city from being destroyed by his own son
that is a very very different kind of leadership than what we saw from absalom is it not
completely different i mean absalom is perfectly willing to split the entire country in half
and start a war just to get power for himself but david is willing to give up all his power
just to save the country that is the absolute key contrast of this entire chapter one man takes
whatever he wants and the other man gives up everything he has so they pack up to leave
and it must have been incredibly chaotic people running around grabbing whatever they can carry
the text notes that david left 10 women his concubines
to stay behind and look after the royal house yes and that is a very fateful

and you have to remember again itai is a foreigner he is from gath he has no reason
to care about israel's throne yet he shows more fierce loyalty more deep faithfulness

itai follows him entirely because of the kind of man david is

of course the king wants god's physical presence to travel with him

behold here am i let him do to me as seemeth good unto him wow let him do to me as seems good to him
that is just that is total absolute surrender it is and this is exactly what the art of
biblical narrative is all about the writer is using this dialogue to show us massive character
so well think about young david years ago david might have tried to cleverly control this whole
situation he might have used the ark to scare absalom's army but now as an older man he is
broken he is humble he is not fighting the reality no he calmly accepts that maybe his time as king

mistakes he does not try to fight god he completely submits he isn't trying to use
god for his own gain anymore he's just blindly trusting god yes but at the same time practically
speaking he is also being incredibly smart here what do you mean well he looks at zadoth the

he realizes he can use the priests so he sends them back into the city to be his secret spies
ah okay so he is deeply submitting to god spiritually

terrible shame you would cover your head to hide your face and you would take off your sandals
so it is an act of total humiliation yes he is actively choosing not to act like a proud king
